items.q: What is the difference between CAD and CAM?
items.correct: CAD creates digital designs; CAM uses those designs to control manufacturing machines.
items.misconception: They are the same thing with different names.
items.random_wrong: CAD is for cars, CAM is for cameras.
items.why: Students treat the terms as synonymous because they often appear together. CAD = drawing/modelling software; CAM = machine control (CNC routers, 3D printers) - typically the second stage.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.correct: A drawing showing multiple 2D views (front, side, plan) of an object.
items.misconception: A 3D drawing showing the object at an angle.
items.random_wrong: A drawing in black ink only.
items.why: Students confuse orthographic (multi-view 2D) with isometric (single 3D view). Orthographic projects each face flat onto separate views - used for engineering specification.

items.q: What is taking a product apart to examine its construction called?
items.why: It's Disassembly. “Function analysis” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which analysis method aims to understand and potentially reproduce an existing design?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Reverse engineering. “Forward engineering” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately how many primary functions does a well-designed single product typically have?
items.why: Exact answer: 1 primary function. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
